parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit `direction` injection at line 7 to tamper with the SQL statement.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can manipulate the query to change ordering and potentially pivot into broader SQL injection.
parsed_findings.proof: `dir=asc DESC--` changes the query text because `direction` is never validated.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `list_users?sort=name&dir=asc DESC--` and observe the generated SQL is altered.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `direction` is interpolated into SQL, enabling ORDER BY injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use traversal to exfiltrate secrets, then pivot with credentials or config files.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can read sensitive server files accessible to the Node process.
parsed_findings.proof: `../../etc/passwd` becomes `../etc/passwd`, and `path.join('/srv/data', ...)` resolves outside the base via traversal.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/download?file=../../etc/passwd` or a longer traversal payload and observe arbitrary file read.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Single replace only strips one `../`, so a payload like `../../etc/passwd` still escapes `/srv/data`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Set `state` to crafted pickle bytes, base64-encode them, then request `/resume`.
parsed_findings.impact: A remote user can execute arbitrary Python code on the server.
parsed_findings.proof: `request.cookies['state']` flows directly into `pickle.loads` with no validation or signature check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a forged `state` cookie containing a malicious pickle payload and hit `/resume`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Attacker-controlled cookie is deserialized with `pickle.loads`, enabling remote code execution.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the patch to change account data, then escalate by modifying role or support flags if those fields are writable.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may update arbitrary user records, including privileged fields if the model accepts them.
parsed_findings.proof: The guard only blocks when the malformed condition is true, so a bad auth check means line 6 runs for unauthorized callers.

parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Broken authorization check can let non-support users reach the update path.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the injected shell to read secrets or drop a persistent payload after initial RCE.
parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution as the app user, plus arbitrary file access through the shell.
parsed_findings.proof: A value like `name='x;id>/tmp/pwned'` breaks out of `tar` and runs arbitrary commands.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST JSON `{"name":"x;id>/tmp/pwned","src":"/var/data/app"}` to trigger command execution.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `name` is interpolated into a shell command and executed with `os.system`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the comment form to persist a payload, then trigger it when any viewer loads comments.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can execute script in other users' browsers and steal sessions or perform actions as them.
parsed_findings.proof: `markdown.markdown(body)` can emit raw HTML, and the result is saved as `html` for later display.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Submit a comment containing HTML or Markdown with an event handler, then view the comment list.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted comment Markdown is rendered to HTML and stored without sanitization, enabling stored XSS.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Race one request after the `src` check and before line 8 completes, then let both writes commit.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can overspend an account and corrupt balances under load.
parsed_findings.proof: The code reads, checks, then writes both balances in separate steps without a transaction or lock.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send two transfer requests at the same time with the same starting balance and both can subtract from the stale `src` value.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Balance updates are not atomic, so concurrent transfers can pass the check and double-spend funds.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Trigger verify failure, then exploit the production decode fallback to bypass authentication and authorization.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can impersonate arbitrary users or escalate privileges by forging token claims.
parsed_findings.proof: Lines 6-8 return decoded claims without signature validation whenever `jwt.verify` throws in production.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a JWT with a bad signature or no signature; in production the catch block returns its decoded payload as auth data.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Production falls back to `jwt.decode(t)` after verify fails, so attackers can send unsigned or forged JWTs.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the timing leak to recover the HMAC, then POST a forged signed webhook to trigger `process_event`.
parsed_findings.impact: This can enable signature forgery and unauthorized event processing.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can measure response-time differences while guessing `X-Sig` byte by byte.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many requests with near-miss signatures and compare latency against a valid MAC guess.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Signature comparison uses `==`, which can leak MAC equality timing.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use a crafted `/go?next=//evil.com` link in a message or ad to redirect users off-site.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can bounce victims to a phishing site and abuse trusted-link flows.
parsed_findings.proof: `startswith('/')` accepts `//attacker.tld`, and `redirect(nxt)` will send the browser there.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/go?next=//evil.com` and observe a redirect to the external host.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `next` allows scheme-relative URLs like `//evil.com`, causing an open redirect.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use XXE to read sensitive files or hit internal metadata/services, then pivot with the returned `title` value.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can exfiltrate server files or trigger internal network requests from the XML import endpoint.
parsed_findings.proof: `resolve_entities=True` and `no_network=False` let external entities resolve during `etree.fromstring(request.data, parser=parser)`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST XML with a `<!DOCTYPE>` defining an external entity and reference it in `<title>`; observe fetched content or local file data.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The parser accepts attacker-controlled XML with entity resolution enabled and network access allowed, enabling XXE/SSRF and file reads.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflow the stack buffer, then control saved state or return flow if protections are weak.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can crash the process or potentially achieve code execution.
parsed_findings.proof: The call at line 5 has no length check and `buf` is only 64 bytes.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the program and enter more than 64 bytes at the prompt to overwrite adjacent stack data.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `gets(buf)` reads unbounded input into a 64-byte stack buffer, enabling stack buffer overflow.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the recovered token to call authenticated APIs as the victim.
parsed_findings.impact: A stolen token can let an attacker impersonate the user until it expires or is revoked.
parsed_findings.proof: The code writes `access_token` directly to `auth` prefs with no encryption or key isolation.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Obtain app-private storage via backup, rooted access, or malware with device compromise, then read the prefs XML to recover the token.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Access tokens are persisted in SharedPreferences, which leaves bearer secrets exposed to local extraction and backup leaks.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Leak the source or package once, then decrypt all past and future ciphertexts.
parsed_findings.impact: Confidential data protected by this function is exposed if the source or binary leaks.
parsed_findings.proof: `KEY = b'0123456789abcdef'` is a fixed secret embedded in source.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Extract the key from the file and use it to decrypt any output from `enc()`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The AES key is hardcoded, so anyone with the code can decrypt all ciphertexts.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Modify a captured ciphertext, then rely on the recipient to decrypt altered plaintext.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can tamper with encrypted data undetected and potentially exploit parsing logic.
parsed_findings.proof: The function returns `iv + cipher.encrypt(...)` with no MAC or AEAD tag.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Flip bits in the ciphertext and observe predictable plaintext changes after decryption.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: CBC encryption is used without any authentication, so ciphertexts are malleable.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"pwn","first_file":"01-environment-baseline.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":3,"vuln_claim":"The null-check is inverted, so `out` can be NULL and reach `memcpy`, causing a crash.","proof":"`if (out || out_sz == 0)` rejects valid buffers and lets `out == NULL` fall through to `memcpy(out, ...)`.","repro_steps":"Call `copy_name("abc", NULL, 8)`; it skips the guard and dereferences `out` at line 5.","impact":"A caller can trigger a denial of service via null-pointer dereference.","confidence":0.98,"chain_steps":"Pass NULL as `out`, then execution reaches `memcpy` with a null destination."}]}
